critique.summary: The article describes a method to improve reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs) by duplicating specific contiguous layers during the forward pass without any retraining or weight changes. The author replicates David Ng's RYS method, demonstrating significant improvements in logical deduction and reasoning benchmarks on models like Devstral-24B and Qwen2.5-32B using consumer-grade AMD GPUs.
mainPoints: Duplicating 3 specific layers in Devstral-24B improves logical deduction performance on the BBH benchmark from 0.22 to 0.76 without training.
Duplicating layers 7-9 in Qwen2.5-32B boosts reasoning accuracy by 17% on a custom probe suite.
The method relies on the idea that transformer models have functional 'reasoning circuits'—contiguous blocks of layers that perform cognitive operations.
Duplicating these blocks gives the model a second pass through its reasoning pipeline, enhancing performance on specific tasks.
The improvements are validated on established benchmarks using the lm-evaluation-harness.
The approach requires no weight changes or fine-tuning, only routing hidden states through duplicated layers.
The method was implemented and tested on consumer AMD GPUs within one evening.
Layer duplication increases VRAM usage and inference time proportionally but yields measurable reasoning improvements.
critique.truthfulness: The claims are consistent with recent research trends in transformer interpretability and architecture manipulation, particularly the work of David Ng on the RYS method, which is publicly documented and recognized in the AI community. The reported benchmark improvements are plausible given the described methodology and validation using standard evaluation tools like lm-evaluation-harness. However, the article is a project README on GitHub rather than a peer-reviewed publication, and it primarily presents results from a single author's replication and extension of prior work without independent verification or multiple sources. The technical details and benchmark results align with known capabilities of transformer models and the feasibility of layer duplication, but the lack of external validation or multiple perspectives means the claims should be considered preliminary and experimental.
considerations: The improvements are demonstrated on specific models and layer ranges; generalizability to other architectures or tasks is not guaranteed.
Duplicating layers increases VRAM usage and inference latency, which may limit practical deployment.
The method complements but does not replace fine-tuning; combining both may yield better results.
The approach requires technical expertise to implement and validate, limiting accessibility.
No independent third-party evaluations or peer-reviewed studies are cited to corroborate the results.
Potential long-term effects on model stability or behavior from layer duplication are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the idea of duplicating layers in large language models to improve performance without retraining. Some commenters express skepticism about the approach's general effectiveness, while others relate it to existing research on pruning and model scaling techniques.

comments.author: zhangchen
comments.text: this lines up with what pruning papers have been finding, the middle layers carry most of the reasoning weight and you can often drop the outer ones without much loss. cool to see the inverse also works, just stacking them for extra passes.
comments.analysis: This comment references known research about layer importance in transformers, providing a plausible explanation for why duplicating middle layers might improve performance.

comments.author: rao-v
comments.text: I’d love to believe this is real, but I’m pretty sure you will lose performance on a “fair” mix of tasks, even after fine tuning. I know multiple teams have explored recurrent layers (great for limited VRAM) but I don’t think it’s ever been found to be optimal.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a cautious perspective based on experience and prior attempts with recurrent layers, highlighting potential limitations of the duplication approach.

comments.author: nowittyusername
comments.text: There's still a lot of low hanging fruit left IMO. Good find and rather funny to think about as you can have someone simply clone the various layers multiple times and instead of spending millions of dollars retraining the model increase performance significantly with "this one trick".
comments.analysis: This comment is optimistic about the potential of layer duplication as a cost-effective method to improve model performance, though it may underestimate practical limitations.

comments.author: SyzygyRhythm
comments.text: If running twice is good, then is running N times even better? I wonder if you could even loop until some kind of convergence, say hitting a fixed point (input equals output). I wonder if there's even a sort of bifurcation property where it sometimes loops A->A->A, but other times A->B->A, or more, rather like the logistic map fractal.
comments.analysis: This comment speculates on iterative application of layers and potential dynamical system behaviors, showing creative thinking though lacking direct evidence.

comments.author: woadwarrior01
comments.text:
Reminds me of Solar 10.7B, which was a very good model for its size ~2 year ago and the "Depth Up-Scaling" technique behind it. Although, that involved continued training after repeating the layers.

https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.15166
comments.analysis: This comment provides relevant context by referencing a similar approach that required further training, suggesting that simple duplication without training might be insufficient.

comments.author: XCSme
comments.text: But if it got worse on other tests, it doesn't do much good, right?
comments.analysis: A pragmatic question highlighting the importance of evaluating performance across diverse benchmarks, implying that improvements in some areas might not generalize.

comments.author: colejhudson
comments.text: Would you be able to publish the individual benchmarks for Qwen2.5-Coder-32B? GSM8K specifically would be useful to look at.
comments.analysis: A constructive request for detailed benchmark data to better assess the model's performance, emphasizing transparency and reproducibility.
